title: SSH Server Hardening
description: Best practices for securing SSH servers against brute force, unauthorized access, and exploitation.
fullExplanation: SSH servers are constantly targeted by automated brute-force attacks. Port 22 receives thousands of login attempts per day on any internet-facing server. Hardening SSH involves disabling weak authentication methods, restricting access, and monitoring for suspicious activity. A properly hardened SSH server with key-only authentication and fail2ban is extremely difficult to compromise.

hardeningSteps.description: Disable password login — require key authentication only
Disable direct root login — use sudo after logging in as regular user
Change from default port 22 — reduces automated scan noise (security through obscurity)
Whitelist specific users — deny all others
Disconnect if not authenticated within 30 seconds
Disable X11 forwarding if not needed
Disable port forwarding on restricted servers

tools.description: Monitors auth logs, bans IPs after N failed attempts. Configurable ban time and threshold.
Send packets to specific ports in sequence to unlock SSH port. Obscures SSH from scanners.
Google Authenticator PAM module adds TOTP to SSH login.
Single hardened SSH gateway — all access goes through it. Audit all SSH sessions.
Certificate authority signs user/host keys — centralized access control without managing authorized_keys files.

keyFacts: Port 22 receives ~1000+ brute-force attempts per day on internet-facing servers
Key-only auth + fail2ban: effectively eliminates brute-force risk
SSH-1 is broken — ensure Protocol 2 only (default in modern OpenSSH)
SSH certificate authority: sign user keys with CA — revoke access by removing CA trust
Idle timeout: ClientAliveInterval 300 + ClientAliveCountMax 2 = disconnect after 10 min idle
